response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a person who spends several hours each day scrolling through social media, I've found that the constant barrage of misinformation can really get under my skin. It's like someone keeps spilling kerosene into my coffee cup—annoying but not helpful for what I'm trying to do or enjoy in life.

When I first noticed all this fake stuff circulating online, it made me feel anxious because everything seemed so uncertain now. But being a practical person with years of experience fixing pipes has taught me that you can't let problems paralyze you; you need to tackle them head-on.

So one thing I do is double-check anything unusual by looking up the facts myself or asking knowledgeable friends—preferably those who are handy like my husband, even though he's not always around digitally. And since we're married and raise four kids together in Chicago, family time means relying on each other for support.

Another important step has been limiting how much time I spend exposed to negativity without filters; maybe that's why being a plumber helps—because it keeps my mind grounded away from the chaos of online content. I've also learned not to believe everything at face value because those numbers just don't add up sometimes.",
  "reasoning_summary": "First, acknowledge personal frustration with misinformation.
Second, relate anxiety stemming from uncertainty due to fake news.
Third, use practical skills (plumbing) as analogy for dealing with problems directly.
Fourth, emphasize checking facts through reliable sources or trusted individuals.
Fifth, incorporate family support system where knowledgeable friends can fact-check.
Sixth, apply coping mechanisms from daily life like limiting exposure time.
Seventh, note that even seemingly factual content requires critical evaluation.",
  "tone": "Conversational yet pragmatic with underlying anxiety about misinformation and a practical approach to dealing with it.",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "None",
  "used_persona_fields":["age", "gender", "sexual_orientation", "occupation", "hobbies"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a person who spends several hours each day scrolling through social media, I've found that the constant barrage of misinformation can really get under my skin. It's like someone keeps spilling kerosene into my coffee cup—annoying but not helpful for what I'm trying to do or enjoy in life.

When I first noticed all this fake stuff circulating online, it made me feel anxious because everything seemed so uncertain now. But being a practical person with years of experience fixing pipes has taught me that you can't let problems paralyze you; you need to tackle them head-on.

So one thing I do is double-check anything unusual by looking up the facts myself or asking knowledgeable friends—preferably those who are handy like my husband, even though he's not always around digitally. And since we're married and raise four kids together in Chicago, family time means relying on each other for support.

Another important step has been limiting how much time I spend exposed to negativity without filters; maybe that's why being a plumber helps—because it keeps my mind grounded away from the chaos of online content. I've also learned not to believe everything at face value because those numbers just don't add up sometimes.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
First, acknowledge personal frustration with misinformation.
Second, relate anxiety stemming from uncertainty due to fake news.
Third, use practical skills (plumbing) as analogy for dealing with problems directly.
Fourth, emphasize checking facts through reliable sources or trusted individuals.
Fifth, incorporate family support system where knowledgeable friends can fact-check.
Sixth, apply coping mechanisms from daily life like limiting exposure time.
Seventh, note that even seemingly factual content requires critical evaluation.
response_parsed.tone: Conversational yet pragmatic with underlying anxiety about misinformation and a practical approach to dealing with it.
